text: Magazine editors in the early twentieth century understood what neuroscientists would later confirm: anxiety and desire are the two emotions that most reliably command sustained attention. Cosmopolitan, founded in 1886, perfected the formula — alternate between making readers feel inadequate and promising a solution, usually purchasable. The genius was structural. Advertising did not interrupt editorial; editorial was designed to prime readers for advertising. The cover image created longing; the article deepened it; the product on the facing page offered relief. Each element primed the next in what media scholars now call an anxiety-desire cascade.
question: What do media scholars call the editorial structure where content alternates between inducing inadequacy and offering relief?
